theory.canonical_statement: Before digital television, every household had a direct window onto one of the most profound phenomena in physics — and no one knew it. The snow on an untuned analogue television screen is not purely electronic noise. Approximately one percent of that static is genuine cosmic microwave background radiation (CMB) — the remnant glow of the early universe, 13.8 billion years old, bouncing off the television antenna and manifesting as random flicker on the screen. Every time you saw TV snow, you were watching the afterglow of the Big Bang mixing with your household electronics.
theory.technical_interpretation: not explained by the standard model of either signal. The TZP hypothesis is that they do — and that the shared features reflect the geometry of the ZPF rather than the specific physical mechanism that generated each signal. --- ## Part Two: Power Spectral Density Analysis ### Comparing the Mathematical Textures The statistical structure of a noise field is captured by its **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** — the distribution of power as a function of spatial frequency (or, for temporal noise, temporal frequency). For a 2D noise field $I(\mathbf{x})$ (an image or a sky map), the PSD is: where $\hat{I}(\mathbf{k})$ is the 2D Fourier transform of the intensity field and $\mathbf{k}$ is the spatial frequency vector. The CMB power spectrum $C_\ell$ (expressed in terms of angular multipole $\ell$) shows characteristic acoustic peaks at approximately $\ell \approx 220, 540, 810, \ldots$ — these a In technical terms, the entry packages the selected formula as the operative carrier, the nearby equations as the progression layer, and the symbol key as the compact interpretation of variables used by the source.
